question_text: According to the source, what is modern AI fundamentally based on?
options: Rule-based expert systems written by programmers
Neural networks trained on massive datasets
Symbolic logic engines that deduce facts
Databases of pre-written answers
explanation: Correct: the glossary defines modern AI as neural networks trained on massive datasets — 'a very sophisticated pattern-matching machine trained on billions of examples.' Rule-based systems and symbolic logic describe older, pre-neural AI approaches, and a database of pre-written answers is lookup, not learning — none of these are how modern AI works.

question_text: In a neural network, what gets adjusted during training so the network can predict outputs from inputs?
options: The number of layers
The input data format
The weights (numbers in the layers)
The output vocabulary
explanation: Correct: a neural network is layers of numbers (weights) adjusted during training until it predicts outputs from inputs. The layer count and vocabulary are architectural choices fixed before training, and the input format is a preprocessing decision — none of them are what training tunes.

question_text: When you ask a local model in Ollama a question, which process is happening?
options: Training — the model updates its weights from your question
Inference — running the trained model to generate an answer
Fine-tuning — adapting the model to your data
Backpropagation — computing gradients for each layer
explanation: Correct: answering a question is inference — running the already-trained model, which is cheap and what Ollama does locally. Training and fine-tuning adjust weights using data plus correct answers (expensive, done beforehand), and backpropagation is the gradient mechanism used during training — none happen when you simply ask a question.

question_text: What does it mean when a model is described as a '7B model'?
options: It uses 7 billion bytes of disk space
It was trained on 7 billion documents
It has 7 billion parameters (weights)
It can process 7 billion tokens of context
explanation: Correct: '7B' counts parameters — the numbers inside the network; more parameters can learn more patterns but need more RAM/VRAM and run slower. Disk size depends on quantization (e.g., Q4 ≈ 0.5 bytes/param), training-document count is unrelated to the name, and context length is a separate spec measured in thousands (e.g., 128K), not billions.

question_text: What single task is an LLM actually trained on, from which grammar, facts, reasoning and coding ability emerge?
options: Answering human-written quiz questions
Predicting the next token given previous text
Translating between languages
Classifying documents into topics
explanation: Correct: an LLM is a neural network trained to predict the next word/token; on internet-scale data this simple objective teaches grammar, facts, reasoning and code — 'a very well-read autocomplete.' Q&A, translation and classification are downstream abilities that emerge (or are added by tuning), not the base training task.

question_text: What was the key innovation of the Transformer architecture (Google, 2017)?
options: Self-attention — every word can directly attend to every other word in context
Convolutions that scan text left to right
Storing all training data for lookup at inference
Running on GPUs instead of CPUs
explanation: Correct: the Transformer's key innovation is self-attention, letting every token directly attend to every other token to learn which words are relevant to each other. Convolutions belong to CNNs (and RNNs scanned sequentially — the thing Transformers replaced), models don't store training data for lookup, and GPU execution predates Transformers.

question_text: In 'The cat sat on the mat — it was comfortable', how does a transformer figure out what 'it' refers to?
options: A grammar rulebook built into the tokenizer
Self-attention scores linking 'it' strongly to relevant words like 'cat'
The model always binds pronouns to the nearest noun
A separate coreference database consulted at runtime
explanation: Correct: self-attention computes a score between each word and every other word, so 'it' attends strongly to 'the cat' — relevance is learned, not rule-based. Tokenizers only split text and contain no grammar rules, nearest-noun binding would wrongly pick 'mat', and there is no external coreference database inside an LLM.

question_text: What happens to content that falls outside an LLM's context window?
options: It is summarised automatically and kept
The model effectively 'forgets' it — it can't be used
It is stored in the model's weights for later
It is moved to a vector database by the model
explanation: Correct: the context window is the model's working memory (e.g., ~128K tokens for qwen2.5:14b); beyond that limit the model forgets earlier content. Automatic summarisation or moving text to a vector DB are application-level tricks the developer must build (like RAG), not native model behaviour, and inference never writes anything into the weights.

question_text: Using the source's rule of thumb, roughly how many tokens is one page of text (~562 words)?
options: About 250 tokens
About 750 tokens
About 1,500 tokens
About 75 tokens
explanation: Correct: the rule is ~1 token ≈ 0.75 words and 1 page ≈ 750 tokens, so ~562 words ÷ 0.75 ≈ 750. The other numbers come from inverting the ratio (562 × 0.75 ≈ 422, nowhere near 250 or 1,500) or dropping a digit — only 750 matches the stated page≈750-token guideline.

question_text: BGE-large uses WordPiece tokenization with a 512-token maximum. What happens to text longer than that limit?
options: It is automatically split into two embeddings
It is truncated — the excess is cut off
The model switches to a bigger tokenizer
An error is raised and nothing is embedded
explanation: Correct: the glossary states BGE-large accepts max 512 tokens per chunk and longer text is truncated. Automatic splitting is something your chunking pipeline must do beforehand (the model won't), tokenizers are fixed per model (BPE/WordPiece/SentencePiece are alternatives across models, not switchable at runtime), and embedding still succeeds — silently losing the tail, which is why chunk sizing matters.

question_text: Why does the project's agent run at temperature 0.1 instead of 0.7?
options: Lower temperature makes responses longer
Near-deterministic output keeps the structured THOUGHT/ACTION format reliable
It reduces GPU memory usage
Higher temperatures are only for image models
explanation: Correct: temperature controls randomness (0.0 deterministic → 1.0+ creative); at 0.1 the model almost always picks the most likely token, so it reliably follows the exact output format the agent's regex parsers need. Temperature doesn't control length or memory use, and it applies to any text generation, not just image models.

question_text: How can a 70B-parameter model fit in ~22GB of RAM, per the source?
options: By deleting 90% of its parameters
By 2-bit quantization — storing each weight at much lower precision
By streaming weights from disk on demand
By compressing the training data instead
explanation: Correct: quantization reduces weight precision — FP16 needs ~2 bytes/param but llama3.1:70b-q2_K at 2-bit squeezes 70B params into ~22GB, trading a little accuracy for a 4-8x size cut. Deleting parameters is pruning (a different technique that changes the model), disk streaming would be far too slow for inference, and training data isn't stored in the model at all.

question_text: In RLHF, what signal teaches the model to become more helpful and safe after base training?
options: More internet text scraped at higher quality
Human ratings of outputs — the model learns to produce higher-rated responses
Hand-written if/then safety rules
Longer context windows
explanation: Correct: RLHF has humans rate model outputs, and the model learns to produce responses that score higher — used by ChatGPT, Claude, and instruction-tuned Ollama models. More pretraining text is just more base training, hand-written rules aren't learning at all, and context window size has nothing to do with alignment. (2026 note: DPO-family methods now largely replace the PPO step.)

question_text: Which statement about Ollama is TRUE according to the source?
options: It requires an internet connection for every query
It exposes a local HTTP API at localhost:11434 and runs GGUF models on your own hardware
It only runs on cloud GPUs
It can train new models from scratch
explanation: Correct: Ollama is a local LLM runtime — HTTP API at localhost:11434, GGUF quantized models, automatic GPU use, no internet required, free. That directly contradicts 'needs internet' and 'cloud only', and Ollama performs inference, not from-scratch training (which takes weeks on thousands of GPUs).

question_text: Why do 'dog' and 'puppy' get very similar embeddings while 'car' gets a very different one?
options: Embeddings encode meaning as coordinates, and similar meanings land close together
'Dog' and 'puppy' share more letters than 'car'
The embedding model was told they are synonyms
Shorter words always get smaller vector values
explanation: Correct: an embedding converts text into a vector representing its MEANING, so semantically similar words get similar coordinates in the space. Letter overlap is irrelevant (embeddings aren't spelling-based), no one hand-labels synonyms — similarity is learned from training data — and word length has no relationship to vector magnitude.

question_text: What is special about how BGE-large-en-v1.5 should be used for retrieval queries?
options: Queries must be lowercase
A query prefix is added: 'Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages: ...'
Queries must be exactly 512 tokens
It requires images alongside text
explanation: Correct: BGE-large uses a special query prefix for better retrieval performance — the model was trained to expect it. Lowercasing isn't required by BGE, 512 tokens is a MAXIMUM not a required length, and BGE is text-only (image embedding is CLIP's job). 2026 note: successors like BGE-M3 top today's MTEB leaderboard.

question_text: Cosine similarity between two embeddings measures what, exactly?
options: The straight-line distance between vector endpoints
The angle between the vectors, ignoring their lengths
The number of matching dimensions
The difference in vector lengths
explanation: Correct: cosine similarity = (A·B)/(|A|×|B|) — it measures the angle between vectors, not their length: 1.0 same direction (identical meaning), 0 perpendicular (unrelated), -1 opposite. Straight-line distance is Euclidean distance (a different metric), 'matching dimensions' isn't how continuous vectors compare, and length differences are explicitly normalised away.

question_text: In vector-space terms, what is 'searching for similar content' equivalent to?
options: Sorting all documents alphabetically
Finding nearest neighbours in the N-dimensional embedding space
Counting keyword overlaps between documents
Compressing vectors until they match
explanation: Correct: semantically similar content clusters together in the vector space (1024-dim for BGE, 512 for CLIP), so search means finding the nearest neighbours to the query's point. Alphabetical sorting and keyword counting are classic non-semantic techniques, and compression (quantization) shrinks storage — it isn't a search method.

question_text: What key property makes CLIP useful for finding a chart image with the text query 'a bar chart'?
options: It runs OCR on the image first
It embeds text and images into the SAME vector space, so matching pairs land close together
It converts images into text captions permanently
It only works if the image filename contains 'chart'
explanation: Correct: CLIP was trained on millions of image-caption pairs so 'a bar chart' (text) and a photo of a bar chart (image) get similar vectors in one shared space — enabling text→image and image→image search. CLIP doesn't OCR or caption images (LLaVA generates descriptions), and filenames play no role in embedding.

question_text: What does the Sentence Transformers library actually do in the project?
options: Runs the chat LLM that writes answers
Wraps pre-trained embedding models like BGE and CLIP behind a simple encode() API
Stores vectors on disk
Splits documents into chunks
explanation: Correct: Sentence Transformers (UKP Lab) provides pre-trained embedding models with a simple API — SentenceTransformer('BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5').encode(['text']) returns a numpy array. The chat LLM runs via Ollama, vector storage is LanceDB's job, and chunking is done by LangChain text splitters — each a different module.

question_text: Put the RAG pipeline in the correct order for answering a user query.
options: Generate answer → retrieve chunks → embed query
Embed query → find similar chunks → add them to the LLM prompt → generate answer
Fine-tune model → embed corpus → answer directly
Send query to LLM → LLM queries the database itself
explanation: Correct: at query time RAG embeds the query, finds similar chunks in the vector DB, adds them to the prompt, and then generates — retrieval happens BEFORE generation (that's the point). Generating first defeats the purpose, fine-tuning is the alternative RAG avoids, and in classic RAG the pipeline does retrieval — the base LLM has no database access of its own.

question_text: Why does the source say RAG beats fine-tuning for keeping an assistant's knowledge current?
options: RAG retrieves fresh content at query time — cheap, dynamic, always current — while fine-tuning bakes static knowledge into weights
Fine-tuning cannot store facts at all
RAG makes the base model permanently smarter
Fine-tuned models cannot run locally
explanation: Correct: fine-tuning is expensive, static and hard to update because knowledge lives in the weights; RAG fetches current content per query. Fine-tuning CAN store facts (that's why it's compared), RAG never modifies the model — it only augments the prompt — and fine-tuned models run locally just fine (Ollama serves them).

question_text: The project chunks documents at 512 words with a 128-word overlap. What problem does the overlap solve?
options: It makes the vector database smaller
Sentences spanning a chunk boundary keep their context instead of being split
It speeds up embedding by 128 words per chunk
It removes duplicate content from documents
explanation: Correct: without overlap, a sentence crossing two chunks gets cut; repeating the last 128 words of chunk N at the start of chunk N+1 preserves context across boundaries. Overlap actually makes storage slightly BIGGER (content is repeated) and adds embedding work rather than saving it, and it introduces intentional duplication rather than removing any.

question_text: What extra ability does section-aware chunking give retrieval results, compared with plain fixed-size chunking?
options: Chunks carry their section heading (e.g., '3. AVAILABILITY'), not just a page number
Chunks become exactly equal in size
It eliminates the need for embeddings
It doubles retrieval speed
explanation: Correct: the improvement splits by document structure first (detecting heading patterns), so each chunk knows which section it belongs to — enabling citations like 'Section: LATENCY' instead of only 'Page 12'. It doesn't equalise sizes (structure-based splits vary), embeddings are still required for search, and it improves answer quality/citations, not query speed.

question_text: Which of these is stored as chunk metadata in the project?
options: The model's temperature setting
source, title, section, page, and content_type
The user's previous questions
GPU utilisation at ingest time
explanation: Correct: each chunk carries source (URL/filename), title, section (nearest heading), page, and content_type (text/image/table) — used for filtering and citations like 'According to Page 12, Section: LATENCY'. Temperature is an LLM setting, past questions live in the semantic cache, and GPU stats belong to the monitoring system, not chunk records.

question_text: Why can't a standard SQL database replace a vector database for semantic search?
options: SQL databases cannot store lists of numbers
SQL engines can't efficiently find nearest neighbours in high-dimensional (e.g., 1024-dim) space
Vector data is too large for SQL storage
SQL is too slow for any search task
explanation: Correct: the bottleneck is the QUERY — nearest-neighbour search in 1024 dimensions needs specialised index structures (like HNSW) that standard SQL engines lack. SQL can store number arrays fine, size isn't the issue, and SQL is excellent at other searches (exact matches, ranges) — just not high-dimensional similarity. (2026 note: pgvector adds exactly this indexing to Postgres.)

question_text: Which combination of properties made LanceDB the project's choice?
options: Client-server design, row storage, SQL joins
Columnar storage, dual vectors per row, hybrid search, persistent files, no server, ~8ms queries
In-memory only, single vector per row, keyword-only search
Cloud-hosted, managed scaling, paid tiers
explanation: Correct: LanceDB is Rust-based with columnar storage, both text_vector AND image_vector per row, native hybrid (vector+keyword) search, durable .lance files, serverless connect-and-go usage, and ~8ms queries vs ~45ms for ChromaDB. The other options describe the opposites: it has no server, isn't in-memory-only or keyword-only, and runs locally for free.

question_text: Why was ChromaDB replaced by LanceDB in the project?
options: ChromaDB lost data on restart
ChromaDB was slower and lacked native hybrid search and dual vectors
ChromaDB required a paid licence
ChromaDB could not run on Windows
explanation: Correct: ChromaDB (SQLite-based) was simpler but slower (~45ms vs ~8ms per query), had no native hybrid search, and no dual-vector support — the three capabilities the project needed. ChromaDB does persist data, is open source (no licence fee), and runs on Windows — those weren't the reasons.

question_text: How does HNSW achieve O(log N) search instead of O(N) brute force?
options: It sorts vectors numerically and binary-searches
A multi-layer graph: start at a sparse top layer and zoom down through denser layers to the neighbourhood
It caches every previous query's results
It reduces all vectors to one dimension first
explanation: Correct: HNSW builds a hierarchy of graphs — like finding a city by continent → country → region → city — navigating from sparse to dense layers, giving O(log N) with ~99%+ accuracy (it's approximate). High-dimensional vectors can't be meaningfully sorted for binary search, query caching is a separate feature (semantic cache), and collapsing to 1-D would destroy the similarity structure.

question_text: What trade-off defines Approximate Nearest Neighbour (ANN) search?
options: It may miss the exact nearest neighbour in exchange for far faster search
It returns more results but uses more memory
It works only on text, not images
It requires a GPU
explanation: Correct: ANN algorithms (HNSW, IVF, FAISS, ScaNN) find vectors that are 'close enough' without checking every one — occasionally missing the true nearest neighbour but staying ~99%+ accurate at O(log N) speed. Result count is a query parameter (k), ANN is modality-agnostic (any vectors), and it runs fine on CPU.

question_text: In the dual-vector schema, why do image chunks get BOTH a text_vector and an image_vector?
options: Two vectors make the row more accurate for keyword search
The text_vector (of the LLaVA description) enables text queries; the image_vector (CLIP) enables visual-similarity queries
The image_vector is a backup if the text_vector is corrupted
LanceDB requires every column to be filled
explanation: Correct: the same chart becomes findable two ways — 'find revenue charts' matches the BGE-embedded description, while uploading a similar chart matches the CLIP image embedding. Keyword search uses neither vector (it uses the FTS index on text), the vectors serve different queries rather than backing each other up, and LanceDB explicitly allows nullable fields (text chunks leave image_vector null).

question_text: What does it mean that LanceDB is 'persistent'?
options: Queries are retried until they succeed
Data is written to disk immediately and survives app restarts and reboots
It keeps a copy of data in the cloud
Vectors are recomputed at every startup
explanation: Correct: LanceDB persists everything to .lance files on disk at write time — there's no in-memory-only mode, so data survives restarts and PC reboots. Persistence is about durable storage, not query retries; everything stays local (no cloud); and vectors are stored, which is precisely why nothing needs recomputing at startup.

question_text: A user asks 'how does scaling work?' and retrieval returns a chunk about 'horizontal vs vertical scale-out' with no shared keywords. Which technique made that possible?
options: Keyword search with stemming
Semantic search via embeddings and cosine similarity
An inverted index
Exact phrase matching
explanation: Correct: semantic search finds content by MEANING — query and chunk embeddings land close in vector space even with zero word overlap. Stemming only matches word roots ('scaling'→'scale' would still need shared terms), inverted indexes power keyword lookup, and exact phrase matching is the strictest keyword approach — all three require overlapping words.

question_text: What is the key WEAKNESS of keyword/full-text search compared to semantic search?
options: It cannot find exact strings like '99.99%'
It misses synonyms and related concepts expressed in different words
It is much slower than vector search
It requires a GPU to run
explanation: Correct: FTS finds documents containing exact words or stems, so synonyms and paraphrases slip past it — the exact gap semantic search fills. Finding literal strings like '99.99%' is FTS's STRENGTH (semantic search struggles there), keyword lookup via an inverted index is very fast, and it's a classic CPU technique needing no GPU.

question_text: For the query 'What is the 99.99% SLA?', why does hybrid search beat either method alone?
options: It runs the query twice for redundancy
Semantic search finds availability concepts while keyword search pins the literal '99.99%' string — fused, you get both
It always returns twice as many results
Keyword search corrects the embedding model's spelling
explanation: Correct: hybrid search fuses vector results (meaning: availability/SLA concepts) with FTS results (the exact '99.99%' token) in one query — each covers the other's blind spot. It's two complementary METHODS, not the same method twice; result count is still controlled by k after fusion; and no spelling correction is involved.

question_text: The project's multi-query retrieval runs three variants of every question. What are they?
options: Three random paraphrases from the LLM
The original question, a key-terms-only version, and a domain-boosted variant
The question in three different languages
Three different embedding models on the same text
explanation: Correct: variant 1 is the original (broad semantic), variant 2 keeps key terms only (focused keyword), variant 3 adds domain vocabulary like 'latency RAM SSD network nanoseconds' (domain-boosted) — catching content phrased differently from the user's wording. The variants are deterministic strategies, not random LLM paraphrases; no translation happens; and one embedding model serves all three.

question_text: In Reciprocal Rank Fusion (score = Σ 1/(rank+60)), what is the purpose of the constant 60?
options: It limits results to 60 documents
It prevents top-ranked items from dominating the fused score unfairly
It converts ranks into percentages
It penalises documents longer than 60 words
explanation: Correct: adding 60 to the rank flattens the curve — rank 1 scores 1/61 vs rank 3's 1/63 — so an item ranked well in SEVERAL lists beats an item ranked #1 in only one; without it, single top ranks dominate. The 60 has nothing to do with result counts, percentage conversion, or document length.

question_text: Without MMR, what typically goes wrong with a plain top-5 similarity ranking?
options: The five results may all say the same thing, wasting context tokens
Results are returned in random order
The most relevant result gets excluded
The LLM refuses redundant input
explanation: Correct: pure relevance ranking often returns five near-duplicates; MMR's formula (λ×relevance − (1−λ)×max-similarity-to-already-selected) selects results that are relevant AND different, covering five angles of the topic. Plain rankings are ordered (not random), the top result is still included (MMR starts from relevance), and LLMs happily accept redundant input — it's just wasted context budget.

question_text: When does table-aware routing intervene in the results list?
options: On every query, unconditionally
When the query mentions 'table', 'chart', 'list' or 'comparison' — table chunks get boosted to the front
Only when zero results are found
Whenever a table exists anywhere in the corpus
explanation: Correct: routing is conditional on query intent — detecting words like 'table'/'chart'/'comparison' and moving content_type='table' chunks to the top so structured questions get structured data. Boosting every query would corrupt ordinary text queries, it's a re-ordering step (not a zero-results fallback), and the mere existence of tables doesn't trigger anything.

question_text: The query 'What is 99.99% availability in minutes?' kept retrieving concept docs while the '52 minutes' table sat at rank 7. How does factual anchor injection fix this?
options: It re-embeds the whole corpus with a better model
It detects the 99.XX% pattern, scores pool candidates for co-occurrence of the percentage with a time value, and injects the best match into results
It asks the LLM to guess the number from general knowledge
It permanently boosts all tables to rank 1
explanation: Correct: it's a post-retrieval correction targeting specific factual patterns — find candidates where the exact percentage co-occurs with a time value and force the best one into the final set, so the LLM sees '52 minutes' instead of inferring. Re-embedding is a heavyweight non-fix for a ranking issue, guessing defeats RAG's purpose, and unconditionally boosting tables is table routing gone wrong — anchor injection is pattern-triggered.

question_text: In two-stage retrieval, why not just run the accurate cross-encoder on the whole corpus?
options: Cross-encoders only handle 10 documents total
Cross-encoders must run the model per query-document pair — O(N) per query — so they're only feasible on a small candidate set
Bi-encoders are more accurate anyway
Cross-encoders can't output scores
explanation: Correct: a cross-encoder reads query+document together, so every candidate requires a full model pass — fine for re-scoring the top ~20 from the fast bi-encoder stage, ruinous for millions of chunks (bi-encoders compare precomputed vectors in ~O(1)). There's no hard 10-doc limit, bi-encoders are FASTER but less accurate (that's the whole trade), and outputting a relevance score is exactly what cross-encoders do.

question_text: What architectural difference makes a cross-encoder more accurate than a bi-encoder?
options: It has more parameters
Query and document interact through attention layers — read TOGETHER, not embedded separately
It was trained longer
It uses keyword features alongside vectors
explanation: Correct: a bi-encoder embeds query and chunk separately and compares vectors, missing nuanced relationships; a cross-encoder feeds the (query, document) pair through the model jointly so every token attends across the pair. Size and training time aren't the defining difference (ms-marco-MiniLM is small), and cross-encoders are neural scorers, not keyword hybrids. 2026 note: BGE-Reranker-v2-m3 and Cohere Rerank 4 are current successors.
